data.title: T1 Energy targets April close for remaining $350M G2_Austin Phase 1 funding while maintaining 3.1-4.2 GW 2026 G1 output
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: T1 Energy Inc. (TE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Barcelo framed the strategy around completing domestic vertical integration, saying, "In 2026, we are building our G2_Austin solar cell fab to complete our vertically integrated domestic solar chain in the U.S. market that completely changed on January 1 with the implementation of new federal rules on foreign content and ownership." He added, "Next year, 2027 is the year we intend to deliver a step-change in our ability to generate earnings and cash flow."

  • Barcelo highlighted Q4 capital formation and commercial steps tied to G2, including "a $72 million registered direct common equity offering," "a $50 million convertible preferred tranche" from Encompass-managed funds, and a December raise "raising combined gross proceeds of $322 million" through concurrent common equity and convertible notes. He said Phase 1 construction at G2 is underway and reiterated the Phase 1 production goal: "Following the completion of Phase 1, we expect to begin producing high efficiency, high domestic content solar cells by the end of this year with an annual capacity of 2.1 gigawatts."

  • Barcelo also pointed to the Treaty Oak Clean Energy deal timing and volume, stating it was "highlighted by a 3-year agreement for T1 to supply 900 megawatts of G1 modules with G2 domestic cells starting in 2027." On the existing Dallas module plant, he said, "quarterly production and sales surpassed 1 gigawatt for the first time" in Q4.

  • CFO Evan Calio described liquidity and funding capacity, saying, "T1 ended 2025 with a much improved liquidity position in a fully ramped factory that hit our production targets" and "we're able to raise more than $440 million in the fourth quarter." Calio said one-time items affected 2025 EBITDA and "account for much of the miss versus guidance," citing several specific drivers.

  • SVP Otto Erster Bergesen said G2 construction and engineering are advancing, and noted the remaining build requirement: "the remaining CapEx required to complete Phase 1, which now stands at $350 million." He added, "we're currently at 90% design" and said T1 expects equipment to arrive "over the summer."

Outlook

  • Management maintained G1_Dallas 2026 production and sales targets, with Barcelo stating, "We are maintaining our production and sales targets of 3.1 to 4.2 gigawatts for G1 in 2026, and we are growing increasingly comfortable with our ability to achieve the high end of that to target range."

  • Calio flagged near-term variables that could shift pricing and timing, including "a ruling in a Section 232 case" and "customer safe harboring activity and projected timelines." He also said the company expects timing shifts between quarters: "we're deferring some 1Q deliveries and expect a significant shift in sales volumes from 1Q to 2Q 26 due to customer requests and timelines," adding, "The shift does not change our expected 2026 revenue or adjusted EBITDA, only the timing."

  • Compared with Q3, the company’s messaging moved from preparing to start G2 construction to stating active build progress and a specific financing timeline. Barcelo said, "we remain in advanced discussions on both fronts and expect to close funding in April," referring to offtake and financing for Phase 1.

Financial Results

  • Calio attributed 2025 EBITDA underperformance primarily to nonrecurring items, saying, "The nonrecurring and unusual items included" an "accounting classification of $34 million sales commission waiver," "Net sales were $16 million lower than expected" tied to year-end regulatory restrictions and 45x retention, "Net sales were $22.7 million lower due to customer offtake true-up," and "we incurred $15 million" of higher-than-forecast tariffs on imported cells.

  • On operating scale at G1_Dallas, Barcelo said, "we produced a total of 2.79 gigawatts of solar modules in 2025, meeting our annual production target," and described a cost and margin improvement path for 2026, stating, "we anticipate that sales and EBITDA will improve each quarter through year-end" based on delivery schedules and "reduced overall costs."

  • No EPS or quarterly revenue figures were explicitly provided in the Q4 2025 transcript, so comparisons to analyst estimates were not included.

Q&A

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked for color on the remaining Phase 1 funding and April timing; CEO Daniel Barcelo responded, "We are confident that it will be in April" and said the company "have passed on certain... higher-cost options" while staying on schedule; CFO Evan Calio added T1 wants flexibility to expand G2 and wants to "maximize... our merchant exposure as we move into the year."

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked to name new large customers and discuss pipeline cadence; CEO Barcelo said, "Treaty Oak did allow for public disclosure of their name. The others prefer confidentiality" and added, "We remain close on a significant contract."

  • Philip Shen, ROTH Capital Partners: asked about monetizing European assets; CEO Barcelo said T1 has "hired [ Pareto ] to start marketing" the Nordic sites and described market pricing as "anywhere from $0.5 million a megawatt to $1 million in megawatt" for power in the Nordic market.

  • Gregory Lewis, BTIG: asked about the shift in IP to Evervault and potential India AD/CVD implications; CEO Barcelo said, "Evervault is a Singaporean entity, and we are licensing from Evervault" and added, "we don't have operations in India"; Chief Legal & Policy Officer Andy Munro said, "what we have with Evervault is simply an IP license" and called it "only reducing the risk that we face going forward on the [ FIOC ] front."

  • Sean Milligan, Needham: asked about reductions to Trina-related service and sales agreements; CFO Calio responded, "we are moving away from service agreements with Trina" and said the changes "saved an estimated $30 million to $100 million at a 3 to 5 gigawatt run rate," later confirming the reduction would be reflected in SG&A.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, concentrated on financing certainty for G2, customer identity/pipeline conversion, compliance/IP structure, and the magnitude/location of cost reductions.

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A on disclosure, with Barcelo stating, "We just can't give too much color" on financing sources and reiterating, "We're committing to April."

  • Versus Q3, management language shifted from readiness and planning (breaking ground “before year-end”) to execution and near-term commitments (April financial close; construction milestones; explicit one-time EBITDA headwinds), while analyst questioning remained focused on compliance, contracts, and funding visibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The G2 narrative progressed from Q3’s plan to "start construction... before year-end" to Q4 reporting Phase 1 is "progressing on schedule" and targeting "full financial close of the remaining $350 million... in April."

  • Guidance framing changed from Q3 emphasis on 2025 EBITDA guidance ($25 million to $50 million) and integrated run-rate EBITDA targets to Q4 emphasis on bridge-year execution, supplier qualification, and quarter-to-quarter timing shifts, with Calio stating timing changes "does not change our expected 2026 revenue or adjusted EBITDA."

  • Q4 included a more detailed explanation of why results diverged from prior expectations, with Calio listing several nonrecurring drivers behind "the miss versus guidance," which was not similarly itemized in the Q3 call.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited regulatory and trade-policy uncertainty as swing factors, including "a potential Section 232 ruling" and ongoing adjustment around "customer safe harboring activity" and "new supply chain restrictions."

  • The company identified near-term execution dependencies for G2, including the need to secure Phase 1 funding and offtake, with Barcelo saying, "our sales and finance teams have been busy working to secure an additional offtake contract and to line up capital formation options" and expecting to close funding in April.

  • Management described 2025 volatility and cost impacts tied to policy changes and tariffs, including "higher-than-forecasted tariffs on imported" inputs and forced year-end actions to retain 45x eligibility.

Final Takeaway

T1’s Q4 call centered on executing the “bridge year” to a domestic-cell step change: management reiterated 2026 G1 production and sales targets of 3.1 to 4.2 gigawatts, described G2_Austin Phase 1 construction as on schedule with 2.1 gigawatts of annual capacity planned, and targeted an April financial close for the remaining $350 million needed for Phase 1. Management also pointed to specific Q4 commercial progress (including the 900-megawatt Treaty Oak agreement starting in 2027) and detailed nonrecurring 2025 headwinds—spanning a $34 million accounting treatment issue, net sales shortfalls tied to year-end regulatory shifts and true-ups, and $15 million in higher tariffs—while emphasizing a cost and profitability improvement path as Trina-related agreements are reduced and G1 operations mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Accenture remains top pick at UBS on valuation discount
data.description:
• Accenture (ACN) is still a top pick at UBS in the business, education, and professional services sector, as the technology consulting firm is trading at a discount to the S&P 500 for the first time in more than 15 years.
  • “With Accenture stock currently trading at a discount to the S&P 500 for the first time in 15+ years with ~12.8x FY2 P/E vs. our target multiple [of] 19.5x, we believe the market is undervaluing ACN’s earnings growth amid macro uncertainty,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients. “Despite bookings execution and FCF acceleration, we see current sentiment-driven dislocation an opportunity as we remain constructive with [a] $320 PT.”

data.title: Tax Foundation: Liberation Day taxes did not make the U.S. economy better
data.description:
A year after U.S. President Trump declared April 2 “Liberation Day” and promised his tariffs would mark the day “American industry was reborn,” none of the administration’s central economic claims have materialized, experts from Tax Foundation said in a recent article.

The tariff regime, which at its peak reached the highest rates since 1911, was struck down by the Supreme Court in February 2026 when justices ruled the authority used to impose them was not authorized.

While Trump claimed up to $18T in new foreign investment would flow into the U.S. and that “jobs and factories will come roaring back,” the data tells a starkly different story, Tax Foundation experts said.

According to their report, actual foreign direct investment totaled just $288.4B in 2025—below the prior 10-year average of $320.7B—and manufacturing employment declined by 89,000 jobs between April 2025 and February 2026, continuing a pre-existing downward trend.

Tax Foundation experts noted that while tariffs generated $264B in customs duties in 2025, this fell far short of the president’s prediction that they would “bring in trillions and trillions of dollars to pay down America’s debt.”

The research shows that net revenue was further diminished because tariffs mechanically reduced income and payroll tax collections, and the federal debt continued to grow throughout the administration.

Contrary to claims that domestic production would deliver “lower prices for consumers,” research from Harvard’s Pricing Lab estimates a 24% tariff pass-through to retail prices, contributing 0.76 percentage points to Consumer Price Index inflation.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell attributed much of the remaining inflation to tariffs, explaining that “these elevated readings largely reflect inflation in the goods sector, which has been boosted by the effects of tariffs.”

The analysis concludes that “the evidence shows the tariffs were not reciprocal,” explaining that rates were not based on actual foreign trade barriers but on synthetic calculations derived from bilateral goods trade balances.

The Tax Foundation noted that U.S. tariff policy changed more than 50 times in a single year, creating volatility and uncertainty that weighed heavily on investment and hiring decisions throughout the economy.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Job openings continue to dwindle in February: JOLTS report
  • Consumer confidence unexpectedly strengthens in March
  • Chicago PMI falls more than expected in March
  • Home-price growth decelerates in January: S&P Cotality Case-Shiller Home Price Index
  • FHFA house price index rises modestly in January

data.title: Infographic: McCormick and Unilever Foods Combine to Create $20B Global Flavor Powerhouse
Appeals court denies effort to redo criminal case against Boeing
data.description:
A federal appeals court on Tuesday tossed out an effort to revive a criminal case against Boeing (BA) over two deadly crashes of 737 Max jets, Bloomberg Law reported. The court rejected a claim by victims’ families that the federal government formed an improper settlement with the aviation giant last year.

A three-judge panel of the 5th US Circuit Court of Appeals in New Orleans determined the complainants didn’t have the legal standing to challenge a deal between Boeing (BA) and the U.S. Justice Department. A federal judge in Texas approved the settlement.

The ruling maintains a $1.1 billion settlement with federal prosecutors that lets Boeing (BA) avoid a criminal conspiracy charge in relation to the crashes of Lion Air Flight 610 in October 2018 and Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 in March 2019. The accidents killed 349 people and were linked to faulty software.

data.title: Aluminum poised for biggest monthly gain in eight years as Iran war disrupts supplies
data.description:
Aluminum futures on the London Metal Exchange are headed for their biggest monthly gain since April 2018, as the Middle East war disrupted supplies and damaged local production facilities, tightening the global market.

The metal (LMAHDS03:COM) exceeded $3,500/ton Tuesday on the LME and is on course for a 12%-plus monthly gain, bucking a broader downtrend for metals in March.

Some 9% of global ​aluminum supply comes from the Persian Gulf, and most companies there have been unable to export the metal beyond the region since Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, which has forced customers to use their inventories, leaving the market with little buffer to cushion any supply shocks.

Over the weekend, Iranian drones and missiles struck plants run by Aluminium Bahrain and Emirates Global Aluminium; the damaged smelter at EGA's Al-Taweelah plant produced 1.6M tons of cast metal in 2025.

Output from Al-Taweelah can be "written off" for the long term, which could flip the market from a supply surplus of 200K tons to a deficit of 1.3M tons next year, Natixis analyst Bernard Dahdah said in a note.

Alcoa (AA) jumped 5.8% in early trading Tuesday, as Jefferies analyst Christopher LaFemina said meaningful earnings upgrades could be coming for the company as aluminum supply disruptions in the Middle East lift metal prices.

"With obvious challenges in shipping alumina through the Strait of Hormuz, additional supply shocks in aluminum could lead to fly-up price spikes," LaFemina said in a note, tapping Alcoa (AA) as the biggest beneficiary within his coverage, although the shares are not immune from recession risk and operating costs are likely to rise given the jump in energy prices.
